sections.heading: Commissary
sections.body: The base commissary is a US military grocery store carrying American brands at reduced prices. Stock up here for familiar staples, American snacks, and US brands not available off base.

contacts.label: Kadena Commissary
contacts.note: Main commissary for Kadena AB. Largest selection on island.

steps: Bring your military ID — commissary access requires SOFA status.
A 5% surcharge is added to all purchases (covers operating costs — no other taxes).
Bag your own groceries or tip the baggers — they work for tips only.
The commissary restocks frequently but popular American items can sell out — shop early in the week.
Check the commissary app (CommissaryLink) for weekly sales and coupons.
tips: Meat, dairy, and produce are significantly cheaper at the commissary than off base.
The commissary does not carry everything — plan to supplement with Japanese stores.
Holiday weeks (Thanksgiving, Christmas) get very busy — shop early.
Commissary hours vary — check the installation website for current hours.

sections.heading: AAFES / Exchange Food Court
sections.body: The Exchange (PX/BX) carries some grocery items, snacks, and household goods in addition to its retail offerings. The food court has familiar US fast food options.
tips: The Exchange carries a limited grocery selection — good for quick pickups between commissary trips.
Tax-free shopping at the Exchange is a significant benefit — take advantage of it.
The food court is great for familiar comfort food during the adjustment period.

sections.heading: Japanese Supermarkets
sections.body: Japanese supermarkets are excellent — fresh, clean, and well-stocked. Prices are reasonable and the produce and seafood quality is outstanding. Most families shop both on base and off.

contacts.label: San-A (サンエー)
contacts.note: Okinawa's largest supermarket chain. Multiple locations island-wide. Best for everyday shopping.

contacts.label: Ryubo (リウボウ)
contacts.note: Mid-range Japanese supermarket. Good selection and frequent sales.

contacts.label: MaxValu (マックスバリュ)
contacts.note: Larger format store. Good for bulk items and household goods.

contacts.label: Nishimatsuya (西松屋)
contacts.note: Baby and children's goods at very low prices. Essential for families with young kids.

tips: Most Japanese supermarkets do not bag your groceries — bag them yourself at the packing counter.
Bring your own reusable bags — plastic bags cost extra at most stores.
Google Translate camera mode works great for reading Japanese food labels.
Fresh fish and seafood at Japanese supermarkets is exceptional quality and very affordable.
Sashimi-grade fish is widely available and safe — a highlight for many families.

sections.heading: Discount Stores
sections.body: Several discount chains offer groceries alongside household goods at very competitive prices.

contacts.label: Don Quijote (ドン・キホーテ)
contacts.note: 24-hour discount store. Groceries, household goods, electronics, and more. Chatan location popular with military families.

contacts.label: Costco Japan
contacts.note: Membership warehouse store. Located in Chatan. US-style bulk buying. Membership required.

tips: Don Quijote is open 24 hours — useful when you need something late at night.
Don Quijote frequently has sales on snacks, drinks, and household items.
Costco Japan requires a membership — bring your US Costco card or sign up for a Japanese membership, which costs around ¥4,840/year.
Costco Japan carries many US brands and bulk quantities — popular with large families.

sections.heading: Japanese Convenience Stores (コンビニ)
sections.body: Japanese convenience stores are nothing like their US counterparts. They are clean, well-stocked, and sell genuinely good food. You will use them constantly.

contacts.label: 7-Eleven (セブンイレブン)
contacts.note: Most common chain. ATMs accept foreign cards. Hot food, fresh meals, bill payment.

contacts.label: Family Mart (ファミリーマート)
contacts.note: Island-wide locations. Great onigiri and hot foods. Free WiFi.

contacts.label: Lawson (ローソン)
contacts.note: Known for baked goods and desserts. Also has bill payment services.

tips: Onigiri (rice balls) are cheap, filling, and delicious — a staple for many military families.
Hot foods at the counter (fried chicken, steamed buns) are freshly made and very good.
7-Eleven ATMs reliably accept US debit and credit cards — use them to get yen.
You can pay utility bills, road tax, and other bills at any convenience store.
Convenience store desserts and pastries are surprisingly high quality.
Most convenience stores have free WiFi — useful before your home internet is set up.

sections.heading: Farmers Markets & Fresh Produce
sections.body: Okinawa has excellent local produce markets with fresh, affordable vegetables, fruits, and local specialties. A great way to experience local food culture.

contacts.label: Makishi Public Market (牧志公設市場)
contacts.note: Iconic Naha market. Fresh fish, meat, local produce. Bring upstairs to have it cooked. A must-visit.

contacts.label: JA Okinawa Farm Markets (ファーマーズマーケット)
contacts.note: Agricultural cooperative markets. Incredibly fresh local produce at low prices. Multiple island locations.

tips: JA Farmers Markets open early and popular items sell out — go in the morning.
Local Okinawan vegetables like goya (bitter melon) and purple sweet potato are worth trying.
The Makishi market is a great experience even if you are just browsing.
Prices at farmers markets are often lower than supermarkets for local produce.

sections.heading: Money & Payment
sections.body: Japan is still heavily cash-based. Always carry yen. Cards are increasingly accepted but smaller stores and markets often require cash.
steps: Get yen from a 7-Eleven ATM — reliably accepts US cards with low fees.
Set up a Charles Schwab or Wise account before you leave the US — both offer free international ATM withdrawals.
Keep ¥10,000–¥20,000 cash on hand at all times for markets and small shops.
IC cards (Suica, ICOCA) can be used at convenience stores and some supermarkets — pick one up at the airport.
tips: Never tip at Japanese restaurants or stores — it can be considered rude.
Consumption tax (10%) is added to most purchases — prices on tags often exclude tax.
Tax-free shopping is available at some stores for SOFA-status shoppers — ask at the register.
Receipts are detailed and always provided — keep them for TRICARE reimbursement if needed.

sections.heading: Finding American Brands
sections.body: Missing specific American foods is common. Here is where to find them.
tips: The commissary is your best source for American brands — shop there first.
Costco Japan carries many US brands including Kirkland products.
Don Quijote often carries imported American snacks and drinks in its import section.
Amazon Japan ships quickly and carries many imported food items.
iHerb ships supplements and health foods to Japan with reasonable shipping costs.
Local Facebook groups frequently post when specific items arrive at the commissary.
